section.title: Water from the Rock
section.sectionRationale: Six weeks of miracles have not settled Israel's heart. Now a dry camp at Rephidim brings a graver test than hunger: no water at all. Israel's demand becomes an accusation against Moses and the LORD Himself, so God commands Moses to strike the rock where He stands, and the place is forever named for Israel's failure, a byword Scripture will not let go of, Psalm 95, Hebrews 3 and 4.
section.thesis: Israel's thirst curdles into an accusation that God intends to kill them; the LORD answers not with judgment but with astonishing grace, standing on the rock Himself so that when Moses strikes it, water flows for an undeserving people, though the place is named for their unbelief rather than for His mercy.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: a waterless camp and an angry quarrel; the LORD's command to strike the rock where He stands; and the naming of the place, Massah and Meribah, a lasting memorial of testing and quarreling.

verses.text: Then the whole congregation of Israel left the Desert of Sin, moving from place to place as the LORD commanded. They camped at Rephidim, but there was no water for the people to drink.

So the people contended with Moses, “Give us water to drink.” 
“Why do you contend with me?” Moses replied. “Why do you test the LORD?”
But the people thirsted for water there, and they grumbled against Moses: “Why have you brought us out of Egypt, to make us and our children and livestock die of thirst?”

units.label: The LORD stands on the rock
units.note: strike the rock, and water will come out

verses.text: Then Moses cried out to the LORD, “What should I do with these people? A little more and they will stone me!”
And the LORD said to Moses, “Walk on ahead of the people and take some of the elders of Israel with you. Take along in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile, and go.

Behold, I will stand there before you by the {{rock|rock}} at Horeb. And when you strike the rock, water will come out of it for the people to drink.” 
So Moses did this in the sight of the elders of Israel.

units.label: Massah and Meribah
units.note: a lasting name for testing and quarreling

verses.text: He named the place {{massah|Massah}} and {{meribah|Meribah}} because the Israelites quarreled, and because they tested the LORD, saying, “Is the LORD among us or not?”

rock.gloss: A rock or rugged cliff face, often a picture of refuge and strength. The LORD stands upon this very rock before it is struck, and Paul later says plainly that the rock Israel drank from was Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4.

massah.gloss: Testing. Built on the same root as the verb rendered test in verse 2, this name marks the place where Israel's demand became, in truth, a test of the LORD's presence and patience.

meribah.gloss: Quarreling, strife. Built on the same root as the verb rendered contended in verse 2, this name marks the place where Israel's complaint against Moses was, in truth, a quarrel with the LORD Himself.

basic.context: Only six weeks after the Red Sea and fresh from the daily miracle of manna, Israel camps at Rephidim and finds no water at all. Their thirst quickly becomes an accusation: Moses, and through him the LORD, must intend to kill them and their children. God's answer is a fresh miracle of grace, though the site is remembered for Israel's failure rather than God's mercy.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history: a specific crisis at a specific camp, resolved by a specific miracle. It is not a general promise that God will always remove hardship on demand. The lasting lesson, drawn out across the rest of Scripture, is about trusting God's presence and goodness in genuine need rather than testing Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The people contended, from the Hebrew riyb, and tested the LORD, from nasah; those same two roots become the place names Meribah, quarreling, and Massah, testing. The rock, tsur, becomes one of Scripture's pictures of God Himself, and later of Christ.
basic.typology: The rock struck so that water flows for a thirsty, sinning people foreshadows Christ struck for sinners so that life flows to those who trust Him; Paul says plainly that the rock was Christ. Massah and Meribah in turn become a byword for hardened unbelief that later Scripture uses as a direct warning to God's people.
basic.christ: Paul identifies the very rock that gave Israel water as Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, since every undeserved blessing in Israel's story flows from what Christ would accomplish at the cross. Jesus later stands and cries out that whoever thirsts may come to Him and drink living water, John 7:37-38, offering Himself as the true and lasting answer to the thirst this chapter only pictured.
basic.god: God does not answer Israel's unbelief with the judgment their accusation deserved; He stands at the point of the miracle Himself and provides water freely. Yet He also lets the place carry a permanent name for their sin, holding together patient grace and unflinching honesty about human unbelief.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:4 names the rock as Christ. Psalm 95:7-9 and Hebrews 3:7-9 use Massah and Meribah as a direct warning against hardened hearts. Numbers 20 records Israel quarreling over water again decades later, this time costing Moses entry to the land. John 7:37-38 has Jesus offer Himself as living water.
basic.oneStory: This is the pattern of the whole wilderness story: an undeserving, complaining people kept alive by nothing but God's provision. The rock struck for water points forward to Christ struck for sinners, so that everyone who thirsts may come to Him and never be turned away.

context.literary: The chapter continues the wilderness testing cycle begun with Marah and manna: complaint, divine provision, and a lesson meant to be remembered. Unlike the earlier scenes, this one closes not with celebration but with a place name that memorializes Israel's failure.
context.historical: Rephidim sits near Horeb, the mountain where Israel will soon receive the covenant. The generation that watched the Red Sea part and ate bread from heaven is the same generation that now accuses God of murderous intent within weeks, a memory Israel's later worship, Psalm 95, refuses to let its descendants forget.
context.canonical: Massah and Meribah become permanent shorthand in Scripture for testing God and hardening the heart, cited directly in Psalm 95 and Hebrews 3 and 4, while the rock itself becomes a lasting image of God as refuge and, in Paul's reading, of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God provides for His people even in their unbelief, while marking the place forever as a warning: this is what happens when a rescued people test the LORD instead of trusting Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical crisis and a specific miracle; it does not prescribe that God will always meet demands made in a spirit of quarreling. What carries forward is the abiding principle, warned against directly in Hebrews 3 and 4: do not harden your heart as Israel did at Massah and Meribah.

debates.question: Why does God command Moses to strike the rock here, when a very similar scene later in Numbers 20 results in judgment on Moses for striking rather than speaking?

views.label: The two events are simply different in God's instruction
views.case: Here God explicitly commands striking; in Numbers 20 He commands speaking, and Moses' sin is disobeying that specific word, not repeating an action once permitted.

views.label: The change highlights a picture already given
views.case: Some see the shift from striking to speaking as reflecting the rock already having been struck once for all in type; Moses' error in Numbers 20 is treating a settled picture of Christ as though it needed to be struck again.

debates.bearing: Either way, the two episodes together caution against reducing God's works to a formula, and show that even Moses, Israel's mediator, needed grace as much as Israel did.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's demand is described with riyb, contend, a term at home in the law courts, as though Moses himself stood accused. Moses turns the charge back: why do you test, nasah, the LORD? Both roots become fixed in the geography of Israel's memory, Meribah and Massah, so that the ground itself preaches against unbelief long after the water has flowed. The tsur, rock, that God stands upon becomes, in the New Testament's reading, nothing less than Christ.

typology.type: The rock struck at Horeb, Exodus 17:6
typology.fulfillment: Christ, struck for sinners so that living water, the Spirit, flows freely to all who trust Him, 1 Corinthians 10:4, John 7:37-39.

typology.type: Massah and Meribah, the place of testing and quarreling
typology.fulfillment: Become a standing warning in Israel's worship and in Hebrews against hardening the heart, urging believers instead to trust the greater rest found in Christ, Hebrews 3:7-4:11.

study.christ: Paul's plain statement, they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and that Rock was Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, reads this whole scene as gospel: undeserved life poured out for a grumbling, guilty people, secured by the One who would one day be struck in their place at the cross. Jesus later stands in the temple and cries out that anyone who thirsts should come to Him and drink, promising rivers of living water to all who believe, John 7:37-38, the true and final answer to Rephidim's dry ground.
study.god: God meets accusation with provision rather than the judgment it deserved, standing at the very point of the miracle so that His own presence, not merely water, is what truly sustains His people. At the same time He does not erase the memory of Israel's unbelief; the place keeps its hard name, Massah and Meribah, a lasting testimony that His patience is real but His holiness is not mocked.

crossReferences.note: Paul identifies the rock that gave Israel water in the wilderness as Christ Himself.
Decades later Israel quarrels over water again at a place also called Meribah; this time Moses strikes the rock in anger against God's word and is barred from entering the land.
Israel's own worship warns later generations, do not harden your hearts as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness.
Applies Psalm 95's warning directly to New Covenant believers, urging them not to repeat Israel's hardness of heart.
Jesus cries out that whoever thirsts may come to Him and drink, offering Himself as the living water this chapter's water from the rock could only foreshadow.

study.oneStory: God provides water for an undeserving, accusing people by standing, Himself, at the point of the miracle, a pattern of grace running through the wilderness story. But Massah and Meribah becomes a permanent warning that trust, not testing, is what God's rescued people owe Him, a warning the New Testament presses home by pointing to Christ, the true Rock, struck once so that living water might flow forever to all who come to Him.

sermon.title: Water from the Rock for Undeserving People
sermon.why: Piper walks through the four scenes of Exodus 17:1-7 verse by verse, showing that God's astonishing grace toward a quarreling, undeserving people is owing to the blood of Christ, and that Paul's identification of the rock as Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:4, is the key to the whole passage.

quiz.q: How did the people respond to having no water at Rephidim?

options.text: They quarreled with Moses and grumbled that he had brought them out to kill them and their children with thirst.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 record their contention and their grumbling, accusing Moses, and so the LORD, of murderous intent.

options.text: They asked Moses respectfully to seek the LORD on their behalf.
options.note: The text describes quarreling and accusation, not a calm, respectful request.

options.text: They blamed the Egyptians for their thirst.
options.note: Their complaint was aimed squarely at Moses and, through him, at the LORD, not at Egypt.

options.text: They rejoiced, trusting that God would provide as He had with manna.
options.note: The opposite is true; they doubted and quarreled despite having just seen God provide manna and quail.

quiz.q: What did Moses say the people were really doing when they quarreled with him?

options.text: They were testing the LORD, not merely quarreling with Moses.
options.note: Moses makes explicit in verse 2 that their complaint against him was in truth a test of God's patience and presence.

options.text: They were simply expressing understandable fear.
options.note: Their fear was real, but Moses names their action directly as testing the LORD, not mere fear.

options.text: They were following God's instructions to seek water.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests God instructed them to quarrel; this was unbelief, not obedience.

options.text: They were praising God for His faithfulness.
options.note: The text describes contention and testing, the opposite of praise.

quiz.q: What did the LORD command Moses to do about the water shortage?

options.text: Take the staff that struck the Nile and strike the rock at Horeb, where the LORD Himself would stand.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 give this exact instruction.

options.text: Pray silently and wait for rain to fall.
options.note: The LORD's instruction was specific and active, striking the rock, not a call to silent waiting.

options.text: Send scouts ahead to search for a hidden spring.
options.note: No search is commanded; the LORD points Moses directly to a specific rock.

options.text: Return the people to Egypt for supplies.
options.note: The LORD's answer moves Israel forward, not back to Egypt.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that the LORD said, 'I will stand there before you by the rock'?

options.text: It shows God's own presence, not merely the water, was the true source of the people's life.
options.note: God places Himself at the very point of the miracle before Moses ever strikes the rock.

options.text: It shows God stayed at a safe distance from sinful Israel.
options.note: The text says the opposite: God draws near, standing directly at the rock.

options.text: It shows the miracle needed no divine involvement, only Moses' staff.
options.note: The LORD's own presence at the rock is central to the miracle, not incidental.

options.text: It shows God abandoned Israel to fend for themselves.
options.note: The opposite is true; God provides generously despite their unbelief.

quiz.q: Why did Moses name the place Massah and Meribah?

options.text: Because the Israelites quarreled and tested the LORD there, asking, 'Is the LORD among us or not?'
options.note: Verse 7 states this reason explicitly.

options.text: Because it commemorated God's provision of water, a name of thanksgiving.
options.note: Moses named it for their failure, not for God's provision; the text ties the name to quarreling and testing.

options.text: Because it was the site where Israel first received the Ten Commandments.
options.note: That event happens later at Sinai, a different occasion from this naming.

options.text: No reason is given for the name in the text.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly states the reason for the name.

questions: Israel's thirst turned quickly into an accusation, 'you have brought us out to kill us.' Where has a real hardship recently tempted you to doubt God's goodness rather than merely His timing?
God stood on the rock Himself before it was ever struck. How does knowing that the cross secures every provision you receive change the way you ask God for help in a dry season?
Moses named the place for Israel's failure, not for God's mercy. Are you more prone to remember your complaints or God's provision when you look back on hard seasons?

Hebrews warns believers, 'today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as at Meribah.' What would it look like this week to trust God in a specific dry circumstance instead of quarreling or testing Him?
Jesus stood and cried out, 'if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink.' What are you currently drinking from instead of coming to Christ for a thirst only He can satisfy?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew verb rendered contend, riyb, is a term used for formal legal disputes as well as ordinary quarrels; Israel is effectively bringing a lawsuit against Moses and, through him, against the LORD.
Behold, I will stand there before you by the rock renders a striking Hebrew construction placing the LORD's own presence at the point of the miracle, before Moses ever lifts the staff.
Massah, testing, and Meribah, quarreling, are twin names built on the two Hebrew verbs used earlier in the passage, so the place itself becomes a permanent, spoken indictment of Israel's unbelief.
